Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered because I'm juggling a lot at 29 with limited resources. I tried to carve out time on weekends and late evenings after work, but with 12 online courses, it was overwhelming. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often try to find practical applications or work through problems physically rather than just reading. Some weeks I'd manage to put in solid hours, other weeks life just got in the way and I'd fall behind.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what was happening in my life outside of school. I'd have bursts of intense study when deadlines were approaching, sometimes cramming in 6-7 hours in a day. But then there'd be stretches where I couldn't touch the material for days because of work or other responsibilities. I tried to set regular times but honestly, consistency was my biggest struggle this semester.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an assignment reminder would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post from a classmate and that would pull me back in. The peer support group I was part of also helped because they'd message me about upcoming tasks. Without those external prompts, I'd sometimes forget to check in for days at a time.
Q4: For a few weeks mid-semester, logging in did become more routine when I connected with some peers who were also struggling. We'd check in with each other and that accountability made me open the LMS more regularly. But when things got stressful with work and my resources were tight, that routine fell apart. It never fully became automatic for me like brushing my teeth or something.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful to me because I could watch, pause, and actually try things out as I learned. I spent over 220 minutes on videos because that format clicked with my kinesthetic learning style. The discussion forums were also helpful when peers explained concepts in different ways than the instructor. Sometimes hearing another student's perspective made something finally click for me.
Q6: I often ignored the supplementary readings and some of the optional practice quizzes. With limited time and 12 courses to manage, I had to prioritize ruthlessly. The readings felt too passive for how I learn best, and I couldn't always access them easily on my phone when I was commuting. I knew they were probably valuable but I just couldn't get to everything.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam, or if I could physically work through it somehow. If I could see immediate practical application or if peers recommended it, I'd engage. Anything that felt like busy work or wasn't clearly connected to my grade got pushed aside because I simply didn't have the luxury of extra time.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones had deadlines attached, so they got prioritized even when I was exhausted. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that would be nice' but then something urgent would come up. The few optional activities I did were ones my peer support group specifically recommended.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and PDFs to watch and read during my commute or when my internet was unreliable. I also used a physical notebook extensively to work through problems by hand since that's how I learn best. Sometimes I'd find YouTube tutorials or free resources online that explained things better than the course materials.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at my kitchen table working through problems in my notebook for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also study with peers in person or over WhatsApp, discussing concepts and quizzing each other. My hands-on practice with real-world applications of the material wouldn't show up anywhere in the system either.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a lecture video once, then watch it multiple times offline over the next few days. Same with PDFs of notes or assignment instructions. My internet isn't always reliable and I couldn't afford to stream things repeatedly, so downloading once and studying offline was my standard approach. The LMS would only show that initial download.
Q12: Around midterms, everything piled up and I hit a wall. Work got demanding, I was stressed about money, and having 12 courses meant something always needed attention. My attendance dropped because I physically couldn't make everything work. I kept pushing through because that's what I do, but engagement definitely suffered during those peak stress periods.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. Fatigue was constant because I was working and studying simultaneously. The confusion from juggling so many courses meant I sometimes opened the wrong materials or lost track of deadlines. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open a video or reading, have it playing or open on my screen, but my mind would be elsewhere worrying about bills or work. There were sessions where I technically 'watched' an entire lecture but couldn't tell you what it covered. The engagement data would show activity but I wasn't really learning in those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments regularly even knowing they were important. Sometimes it was because I felt overwhelmed and didn't know where to start. Other times I was prioritizing courses where I felt I had a better chance of passing. The anxiety of starting something I might fail at made avoidance feel easier in the moment, even though I knew it was hurting me.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without a deadline approaching, I'd let things slide while dealing with more immediate life demands. When deadlines hit, I'd have these intense bursts of activity, logging in multiple times, rushing through materials. It wasn't healthy but it was the only way I could function with everything else going on.
Q17: Feedback had a big impact when I actually received it. Positive feedback from peers or instructors would energize me and make me want to engage more. But sometimes feedback came too late to be useful, or I was already so behind that it felt discouraging. The peer support intervention really helped because I got more immediate, informal feedback from classmates.
Q18: The course structure was challenging for me because it seemed designed for students with more time and fewer responsibilities. The pacing assumed consistent weekly engagement, but my life doesn't work that way. When modules built on each other, falling behind in one week created a snowball effect. More flexibility in deadlines would have helped someone in my situation.
Q19: Having video content made engagement easier because I could learn on my own schedule. The discussion forums were also good for connecting with peers. But the sheer volume of content across 12 courses made it hard to engage deeply with anything. Clearer prioritization of what was essential versus supplementary would have helped me focus my limited time better.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's trying hard but struggling to complete quality work. You might be logging in, watching videos, reading materials, genuinely trying to learn, but then life gets in the way of actually finishing assignments. Or the assignments might be too difficult given your understanding level, so you engage with materials but can't produce the required output.
Q21: That definitely resonates with my experience in some ways. A lot of my actual learning happened offline with downloaded materials and physical practice. Someone could be deeply engaged with course content but doing most of their studying in ways the LMS can't track. Performance depends on understanding, not just clicking around in a system.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're logging in and what you're clicking on, which gives a basic picture of activity patterns. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline studying that happens. They can't measure whether you're actually understanding material or just going through motions. They also miss the context of why someone might be struggling.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively wrestling with the material, trying to understand it and apply it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through content. For me, engagement is when I'm in my notebook working through problems, or when I'm in a discussion with peers really trying to figure something out. It's a mental and sometimes physical investment in learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the late nights studying offline, the peer support conversations that kept me going, or the personal growth I experienced even while failing. It wouldn't capture that I'm a 29-year-old with limited resources who's still trying to better himself through education despite the odds.
